lesson:
At word 184 you met 閉しめる — closing things with mechanisms: doors, windows, lids. 閉とじる is its gentler sibling. It closes things that fold, that come together naturally: 本ほんを閉とじる (close a book). 目めを閉とじる (close your eyes). 口くちを閉とじる (close your mouth — also shut up).

The distinction: 閉しめる requires effort, a physical act against resistance. 閉とじる is something coming together, returning to a resting state. You 閉しめる a heavy door; you 閉とじる a laptop. Eyes don't have hinges — they 閉とじる.

In figurative use: 幕まくを閉とじる (bring down the curtain — end something). 歴史れきしに幕まくを閉とじる (close a chapter in history). The intransitive form 閉とじる can also be used intransitively — eyes can 閉とじる on their own.

Particles を for what you close: 本ほんを閉とじる.

一段 Ichidan他動詞
Dictionaryとじるを
Negativeとじないを
Politeとじますを
Polite negとじませんを
Pastとじたを
Polite pastとじましたを
て-formとじてを
Volitionalとじようを
Polite volとじましょうを
Passiveとじられるに
Causativeとじさせるに／を
Caus-passとじさせられるに
Potentialとじられるが
Imperativeとじろを
Imp なさいとじなさいを
Cond ～ばとじればを
Cond ～たらとじたらを

時とき is one of those words that does double duty so naturally you barely notice the shift. As a standalone noun: その時とき (at that time, that moment). As a grammar word: 日本にほんに行いった時とき (when I went to Japan). The second use is everywhere — it's how you say "when" for specific situations, as opposed to the question word いつ.

The kanji 時じ pairs 日にち (sun) with 寺じ (temple — here serving as the phonetic component). The sun moving past the temple — time passing, marked by light.

子供こどもの時とき (when I was a child). 困こまった時とき (when in trouble). 時ときが来きた (the time has come). In compounds it becomes じ: 時間じかん (time, hour), 時代じだい (era, period), 時計とけい (clock — this one keeps the と reading).

In oracle bone script, 米こめ was six grains scattered across a cross-shaped separator — rice being sorted after threshing. The modern character still holds that scattered-grain look. It's the grain in its raw form: uncooked, unhusked, still in the bag.

Japanese has different words for rice at different stages. 米こめ is the uncooked grain you buy at the store. 御飯ごはん is cooked rice (and also "meal" more broadly). 稲いね is the rice plant growing in a paddy. English has one word for all of these; Japanese has at least three because rice isn't just food — it's infrastructure.

米こめを研とぐ (wash/rinse rice — the step before cooking). 米こめを炊たく (cook rice). In compounds: 米国べいこく (America — literally "rice country," named from a phonetic abbreviation of 亜米利加). 新米しんまい (new rice — also slang for a newcomer/beginner).

切きる cuts through everything — physical objects and abstract boundaries. 紙かみを切きる (cut paper). 髪かみを切きる (cut hair). 電話でんわを切きる (hang up the phone — cut the call). エアコンを切きる (turn off the AC — cut the power).

The kanji 切せつ puts 七なな next to 刀かたな (sword/blade). A blade dividing — clean separation. In its oldest form, the left component may not have been "seven" but rather a mark indicating the cutting point.

As a verb suffix, 〜切きる means to complete something entirely: 食たべ切きる (eat it all up). 走はしり切きる (run all the way, see it through). 使つかい切きる (use it all up). The image: you cut all the way through, nothing left.

Particles を for what you cut: 野菜やさいを切きる.

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryきるを
Negativeきらないを
Politeきりますを
Polite negきりませんを
Pastきったを
Polite pastきりましたを
て-formきってを
Volitionalきろうを
Polite volきりましょうを
Passiveきられるに
Causativeきらせるに／を
Caus-passきらせられるに
Potentialきれるが
Imperativeきれを
Imp なさいきりなさいを
Cond ～ばきればを
Cond ～たらきったらを

楽たのしい is what you feel during the experience. Not happiness exactly — that's 嬉うれしい. Not interesting — that's 面白おもしろい. 楽たのしい is the specific pleasure of doing something and enjoying it in the moment. A party, a trip, a game, a conversation that's going well. The verb form 楽たのしむ makes it active: to enjoy, to have fun with.

The kanji 楽らく originally depicted a type of stringed instrument — drums on a rack, or bells on a wooden frame (interpretations vary). Music. From the pleasure of music came two branching meanings: enjoyment (たのしい) and ease/comfort (楽らく, らく). 楽らくな仕事しごと (easy work). 気楽きらく (carefree). Enjoyment and ease — related, but not the same.

旅行りょこうは楽たのしかった (the trip was fun). 楽たのしみにしている (I'm looking forward to it — literally "I'm in a state of enjoyment-anticipation"). That last phrase is everywhere in emails and conversation as a way to express anticipation.

Dictionaryたのしい
Negativeたのしくない
Pastたのしかった
Past negたのしくなかった
Adverbたのしく
て-formたのしくて

服ふく is the general word for clothing — what you wear from the neck down. 服ふくを着きる (put on clothes / wear clothes). 服ふくを脱ぬぐ (take off clothes). 新あたらしい服ふくを買かった (bought new clothes).

The kanji 服ふく has an older meaning of "to submit, to serve" — it originally depicted kneeling in obedience. Clothing entered the picture because what you wore signaled your role and rank. Uniforms, court dress, ceremonial garments — clothes as submission to social order. The character still carries both meanings: 制服せいふく (uniform — literally "system clothes"), 服従ふくじゅう (obedience), 服ふくを飲のむ... wait, that last one: 薬くすりを服用ふくようする (take medicine — the "submit/accept" meaning, not the clothing one).

洋服ようふく (Western-style clothes) vs. 和服わふく (Japanese-style clothes, i.e. kimono). 私服しふく (plain clothes, casual wear — what you choose as opposed to a uniform).

後うしろ is the physical space behind you. 後うしろを見みて (look behind you). 後うしろに誰だれかいる (someone's behind you). 後うしろの席せき (the seat behind). It's concrete, spatial — not abstract like "afterward."

The kanji 後ご is one of the most overloaded characters in Japanese. As うしろ, it means physical space behind. As あと, it means "after" or "later" or "remaining": 後あとで (later). As ご in compounds: 午後ごご (afternoon), 最後さいご (last, final). As こう: 今後こんご (from now on). Four readings, each pulling the character in a different direction.

For 後うしろ specifically: it's purely positional. The back of a building. The area behind a person. Where you can't see without turning around. 後うしろ姿すがた (a person seen from behind — their retreating figure) is a common literary image in Japanese.

嬉うれしい is happiness with a cause. Something good happened, and you're glad about it. プレゼントをもらって嬉うれしい (I'm happy to receive a gift). 会あえて嬉うれしい (glad to meet you — genuinely, not formulaic). It's reactive — something triggered this feeling.

Contrast with 楽たのしい (word 205, fun — pleasure during an activity) and 幸しあわせ (happiness as a state of being, contentment). 嬉うれしい is the spike — the moment of glad surprise. 合格ごうかくして嬉うれしい (I passed and I'm thrilled).

The kanji 嬉き has 女おんな (woman) on the left and 喜き (joy) on the right. The 喜き component appears in 喜よろこぶ (to rejoice), a close synonym that works as a verb rather than an adjective. You 喜よろこぶ with visible reaction; you feel 嬉うれしい internally.

Dictionaryうれしい
Negativeうれしくない
Pastうれしかった
Past negうれしくなかった
Adverbうれしく
て-formうれしくて

腰こし covers the zone from your lower back around to your hips — the body's center of gravity. It's broader than English "waist" (which implies a narrow band) and more specific than "back" (which could be anywhere from shoulders to tailbone).

腰こしが痛いたい (my lower back hurts) — one of the most common complaints in Japan, where sitting on floors and long commutes take their toll. 腰こしを下おろす (sit down — literally "lower your hips"). 腰こしを上あげる (stand up, get moving — literally "raise your hips").

The kanji 腰よう combines 月にくづき (flesh radical — body part) with 要よう (essential, important). The essential part of the body — the point everything hinges on. And 要よう itself depicts hands on a waist, so the connection runs deep.

Figuratively: 腰こしが重おもい (slow to act — heavy-hipped, reluctant to get up). 腰こしが低ひくい (humble, modest — low-hipped, bowing deeply). 腰こしを据すえる (settle in, commit seriously — plant your hips).

Japanese weekday names follow the same planetary system as English, through a shared origin in ancient astrology. 日にち = sun, so 日曜日にちようび = Sun-day. The full set:

月曜日げつようび (Mon — moon)
火曜日かようび (Tue — fire/Mars)
水曜日すいようび (Wed — water/Mercury)
木曜日もくようび (Thu — wood/Jupiter)
金曜日きんようび (Fri — metal/Venus)
土曜日どようび (Sat — earth/Saturn)
日曜日にちようび (Sun — sun)

English uses Norse gods (Tuesday = Tyr's day, Wednesday = Woden's day). Japanese uses the five Chinese elements plus sun and moon. Different mythologies, same planetary assignments underneath.

The 曜よう character specifically means "day of the week" in this context. 何曜日なんようび (what day of the week?). In casual speech, the final 日び is often dropped: 日曜にちように会あおう (let's meet Sunday).

You already met 近ちかい — the adjective meaning "close." 近く is what happens when you shift it into noun/adverb territory. Drop the い, add く, and the word stops describing something and starts being a place or a time: 駅えきの近ちかく (near the station — a location). 近ちかくに住すんでいる (I live nearby).

The time meaning sneaks up on people. 近ちかく届とどくと思おもいます (I think it will arrive before long). Here 近く doesn't point to a physical spot — it points to a temporal one, the near future. Same logic: something is close, whether in space or in time.

This noun/adverb shift — い-adjective → く form as a standalone word — is a pattern worth watching for. 遠い → 遠く (far away, a distant place). 多い → 多く (many, a large number). The く form doesn't always become an independent word, but when it does, it's always a noun describing the quality itself or the place/amount defined by it.

甘い means sweet — but Japanese sweetness has a longer reach than English. Sugar is 甘い. A parent who spoils a child is 甘い. A grading rubric that lets everyone pass is 甘い. An overly optimistic plan is 甘い. The thread connecting them: softness where there should be firmness. Too easy, too lenient, too indulgent. When someone says 考かんがえが甘あまい (your thinking is naive), they're saying you've sugared over the hard parts.

甘あまえる — the verb form — is one of those Japanese concepts with no clean English equivalent. It means to depend on someone's kindness, to act spoiled, to let yourself be indulged. Babies 甘える with their mothers. Adults sometimes 甘える with partners. The psychologist Takeo Doi wrote an entire book (The Anatomy of Dependence) arguing that 甘え is the key to understanding Japanese social behavior.

The kanji 甘 is thought to depict something held in the mouth — a horizontal line inside the mouth radical. Taste it and know it. Sweet on the tongue, soft at the core.

Dictionaryあまい
Negativeあまくない
Pastあまかった
Past negあまくなかった
Adverbあまく
て-formあまくて

Japanese had tables long before this word arrived. The ちゃぶ台だい — a low table you sit around on the floor — is so culturally iconic that there's a gesture named after it: ちゃぶ台返だいがえし (flipping the table in anger), immortalized in the anime Star of the Giants and still used as a metaphor for losing your temper and destroying everything. テーブル is its Western replacement — a table with legs, paired with chairs instead of floor cushions. When Japan imported Western furniture during modernization, the word came with it.

The split tells you about the setting. A ちゃぶ台 or 卓たく lives in a tatami room. A テーブル lives in a Western-style room with a hard floor. In compound territory, the native word still dominates: 食卓しょくたく (dining table), 卓球たっきゅう (table tennis — literally "table ball"). テーブル is the loanword that marks which era of furniture you're sitting at.

A compound you can decode on sight: 食たべ (eat, from 食たべる) + 物もの (thing) = eating-thing = food. This 〜物 pattern is productive. 飲のみ物もの (drink), 買かい物もの (shopping), 忘わすれ物もの (forgotten item). Verb stem + もの = the thing associated with that action. Once you see the pattern, you can often guess new compounds before looking them up.

食べ物 vs ご飯 vs 料理: Three words for "food" that aren't interchangeable. 食べ物 is the broadest — anything edible, food as a category. ご飯はん means a meal or cooked rice — it's what you sit down to eat. 料理りょうり is prepared cuisine, food as something cooked with skill. 好すきな食たべ物ものは？ (what food do you like?) is asking about your taste in general. ご飯はん食たべた？ (did you eat?) is asking about a specific meal.

You already know 始はじめる — "to start something." 始まる is its intransitive partner: things start on their own. 映画えいがが始はじまる (the movie starts). 授業じゅぎょうが始はじまった (class has begun). Nobody starts the movie in this sentence — it just begins. That's the whole difference: 始める needs someone pulling the trigger. 始まる just happens.

This める/まる pair is one of the most common transitive/intransitive patterns in Japanese. 決める/決まる (decide/be decided), 集める/集まる (gather something/things gather). If you see a verb ending in める, look for a まる partner — chances are good it exists.

Particles: 始まる takes が — the thing that begins is the subject, not an object you're acting on. 会議かいぎが始はじまる (the meeting begins). Nothing starts it; it starts.

Passive が始はじまられる: extremely rare for intransitive 始まる. If you need passive, use 始められる (passive of transitive 始める) instead.

Causative を／に始はじまらせる: also rare. Japanese naturally uses 始めさせる (causative of 始める) when you want to make someone start something.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 始める (はじめる)
Dictionaryはじまるが
Negativeはじまらないが
Politeはじまりますが
Polite negはじまりませんが
Pastはじまったが
Polite pastはじまりましたが
て-formはじまってが
Volitional
Polite vol
Passive
Causative
Caus-pass
Potentialはじまれるが
Imperativeはじまれが
Imp なさいはじまりなさいが
Cond ～ばはじまればが
Cond ～たらはじまったらが

From English "game," but in Japanese ゲーム has drifted toward a narrower default. Say ゲーム without context and most Japanese people will picture a video game first, a board game second, and a sports match barely at all. For sports, Japanese uses 試合しあい. For children playing, 遊あそび. ゲーム has carved out its own territory: structured play with rules, especially electronic.

ゲームをする (play a game). ゲームセンター (game center — arcade). ゲーム機き (game console — literally "game machine"). Japan's relationship with games needs no introduction — the country that produced Nintendo, Sony PlayStation, and most of the RPGs the world grew up on. When a Japanese person says ゲームばかりしている (all they do is play games), it's a complaint you'll hear from parents and partners across the country.

A cross: 十. One horizontal line, one vertical. The simplest kanji in the entire writing system. Why a cross for ten? In ancient Chinese tally systems, ten was marked with a vertical stroke through a horizontal one — a complete set, all fingers counted. The character has barely changed in three thousand years because there's nothing to simplify.

じゅう is the Chinese reading. You'll also encounter the native Japanese とお for the standalone number ten, though じゅう dominates in compounds and counting: 十一じゅういち (eleven), 十分じっぷん (ten minutes — note the sound change), 二十にじゅう (twenty). The whole number system above ten is just stacking: 二十 = 2×10, 三十 = 3×10, 百 (100), 千 (1000). Ten is the first building block you need.

Sound trap: 十 before certain consonants compresses to じっ or じゅっ: 十分じっぷん can be じっぷん or じゅっぷん (both accepted). 十回じっかい (ten times). This is just Japanese smoothing out awkward consonant clusters — say じゅうかい fast enough and it naturally collapses.

天 (heaven, sky) + 気 (spirit, air, energy) = the mood of the sky. Japanese packages weather not as a meteorological report but as the sky's temperament on a given day. 天気てんきがいい (the weather is good). 天気てんきが悪わるい (the weather is bad). Notice the adjectives: good and bad, not warm and cold. 天気 gets judged morally, like a person's mood.

Weather talk in Japanese is as socially essential as it is in English. いい天気てんきですね is the Swiss Army knife of small talk — safe, universally usable, and signals that you're a normal human who acknowledges shared experience. If you're ever stuck for a conversation opener, this is it.

The kanji 天 is a person (大) with a line above their head — what's above a person is the sky, the heavens. 気 originally depicted steam rising from cooking rice — visible energy, breath, atmosphere. Weather is the energy radiating from the heavens. The etymology lines up.

Japanese has three different あつい — three words that sound identical but mean completely different things, written with different kanji. This is the one that'll matter most from June through September.

暑い — hot weather. The air around you is hot. 今日きょうは暑あつい (it's hot today).
熱い — hot to touch. A surface, a liquid, a thing that burns. このコーヒーは熱あつい.
厚い — thick. A book, a wall, a steak. Completely unrelated meaning, same sound.

The kanji tells you which is which. 暑 has 日 (sun) at the top — the sun beating down, environmental heat. 熱 has the fire radical — direct, physical heat from a source. If you're complaining about summer, you want 暑い. If you're warning someone about their soup, you want 熱い. Mix them up and people will know exactly what you mean, but the kanji distinction reveals how Japanese carves up the concept of heat differently from English.

Dictionaryあつい
Negativeあつくない
Pastあつかった
Past negあつくなかった
Adverbあつく
て-formあつくて

太い describes girth — something that's wide around, not just big. A thick rope, a fat marker pen, a stout tree trunk. 太ふとい腕うで (thick arms). 線せんが太ふとい (the line is thick). It's physical bulk measured across, not up or out.

The kanji 太 is 大 (big) with a dot added. That single dot is the difference between "big" and "thick" — a small addition that changes the shape of bigness from general to specifically round and full. The verb 太ふとる (to gain weight) comes from the same root, and it's the word you'd use for a person getting heavier. Calling a person 太い directly is blunt — it's closer to "fat" than "heavyset." Like in English, there are gentler ways to say it, and native speakers usually choose them.

太い vs 厚い vs 細い: Dimensions in Japanese get specific. 太い is thick around (circumference). 厚あつい is thick through (depth — a thick book, a thick wall). 細ほそい is the opposite of 太い — thin, slender, narrow.

Dictionaryふとい
Negativeふとくない
Pastふとかった
Past negふとくなかった
Adverbふとく
て-formふとくて

晩 is evening — specifically the early-to-mid evening, the hours after work when you eat dinner and wind down. It overlaps with 夜よる (night) but skews earlier. 今晩こんばん (tonight/this evening) is one of the first compounds you'll use: こんばんは (good evening). 晩ばんご飯はん is dinner — the evening meal.

The kanji 晩 has 日 (sun) on the left and 免 on the right. The sun is departing, slipping away. That trailing quality is baked into how 晩 feels — not the deep dark of 夜, but the transition into it. In literary Japanese, 晩年ばんねん means the later years of someone's life, the evening of their time. Same word, scaled up from hours to decades.

土 (earth, soil) + 曜日 (day of the week) = Earth-day = Saturday. The Japanese days of the week follow the same celestial body system as the Western ones, which shouldn't be a coincidence — it isn't. Both trace back to the same Hellenistic astrological tradition that assigned planets to days. The system entered Japan via China around the 9th century.

Saturday = Saturn's day in English, and Saturn was associated with the element earth in East Asian cosmology. So 土曜日 and "Saturday" are distant cousins, connected through the same planet. The full set: 日 (Sun), 月 (Moon), 火 (Mars/fire), 水 (Mercury/water), 木 (Jupiter/wood), 金 (Venus/metal), 土 (Saturn/earth).

土 as a kanji is a cross planted in the ground — a stake marking where the earth is. It's one of the five classical elements, and you'll see it in 土地とち (land), 土産みやげ (souvenir — literally "earth-product," a gift from the place you visited). Saturday carries that grounded energy.

The word you'll need at the doctor, the dentist, and the gym. 頭が痛い (my head hurts). お腹が痛い (my stomach hurts). The pattern is always [body part] が 痛い — the body part is the grammatical subject, the thing that's in a state of pain. Not "I have a headache" but "my head is painful." Japanese centers the body part, not the sufferer.

Beyond physical pain, 痛い has developed a slang meaning in modern Japanese that's worth knowing: embarrassingly clueless. あの人、ちょっと痛いよね (that person is kind of cringe). Someone who reads the room wrong, who tries too hard, who doesn't realize how they come across — that's 痛い. The logic: watching them makes you wince, as if in pain.

The kanji 痛 has the sickness radical 疒 (that slanted roof with dots — you'll see it on many medical/health kanji) enclosing 甬, a phonetic component. Any time you see 疒 wrapping a kanji, expect something related to illness or physical distress.

Dictionaryいたい
Negativeいたくない
Pastいたかった
Past negいたくなかった
Adverbいたく
て-formいたくて

The polite, everyday word for father — what you'd call your own dad at home, and what you'd use when referring to someone else's father in conversation. お (honorific prefix) + 父さん. Compare with 父ちち, the humble form you'd use when talking about your own father to outsiders. This inside/outside distinction runs through all Japanese family terms: お母さん/母, お兄さん/兄. You elevate their family, you humble your own.

But here's what trips people up: you also call your own father お父さん when speaking to him or about him within the family. お父とうさん、ご飯はんできたよ (Dad, dinner's ready). It's not humble or polite in that context — it's just what "Dad" sounds like in Japanese. The humble form 父 only activates when you're talking about him to outsiders: 父ちちは会社員かいしゃいんです (my father is a company employee).

Younger kids often say パパ. Rougher, more masculine speech uses 親父おやじ — closer to "old man" in tone.

多分 sits at about 70-80% certainty. Not "definitely." Not "who knows." It's the word for when you're fairly sure but don't want to commit. 多分たぶん大丈夫だいじょうぶ (probably fine). 多分たぶん来くると思おもう (I think they'll probably come).

The kanji literally says 多 (many) + 分 (parts) — "for the most part." That etymology maps perfectly onto the meaning: the majority of the evidence points this way, but not all of it. It's hedged confidence.

Certainty scale: Japanese has a gradient of commitment that's useful to place 多分 on. From most to least certain: 絶対ぜったい (absolutely) → きっと (surely, ~90%) → 多分 (probably, ~70%) → もしかしたら (possibly, ~30%). Japanese conversation lives in the 多分 zone — definitive statements feel aggressive, so speakers naturally gravitate toward this comfortable middle ground.

時 (time) + 計 (measure, count) = a device that measures time. Wall clock, wristwatch, alarm clock — all 時計. Unlike English, which splits these into separate words, Japanese uses one and lets context or a prefix sort it out: 腕時計うでどけい (wristwatch — arm-clock), 目覚まし時計めざましどけい (alarm clock — wake-up clock), 置き時計おきどけい (desk clock — placed clock).

Notice the voicing: standalone it's とけい, but in compounds the と softens to どけい. This is 連濁 (rendaku) — the same sound-smoothing process that turns 花はな into 生け花いけばな. No rule governs when rendaku happens and when it doesn't. It's one of those things you absorb through exposure.

The reading とけい is a slightly unusual compound reading — you might expect じけい from the standard on'yomi. The word's pronunciation came from a different historical path and just stuck.

Three verbs pronounced とまる. Three different kanji. Three different meanings. This is the one about sleeping somewhere that isn't your home.

泊まる — to stay overnight (at a hotel, a friend's place). Has the water radical 氵, originally connected to boats mooring in a harbor — a vessel stopping for the night.
止まる — to stop, cease (movement ends).
停まる — to stop (vehicles pulling over, parking).

When you hear とまる, the overnight meaning is usually clear from context — hotels, friends' houses, travel plans. ホテルに泊まる (stay at a hotel). 友達の家に泊まる (stay at a friend's house). The particle に marks where you stay — the destination of your overnight presence.

Particles: 泊まる takes に — the place you stay at. 旅館に泊まる (stay at a ryokan). You can add a duration with counter: 二泊する (stay two nights) uses the noun form 泊はく with する.

Passive に泊まられる: someone staying at your place uninvited or inconveniently. 友達に三日も泊まられた (my friend stayed over for three whole days — and I had to deal with it). Classic suffering passive.

Causative を／に泊まらせる: letting or making someone stay over. に for permissive: 友達に泊まらせる (let a friend stay over).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 泊める (とめる)
Dictionaryとまるに
Negativeとまらないに
Politeとまりますに
Polite negとまりませんに
Pastとまったに
Polite pastとまりましたに

どうして literally breaks down as どう (how) + して (doing) — "doing how?" → "how did this come about?" → "why?" The path from "how" to "why" is a short one: if you're asking how something happened, you're really asking why it happened. English does this too — "how come?" means "why?"

Three ways to ask "why" in Japanese, each at a different register:
なぜ — formal, written, stiff. News reports, academic papers.
どうして — middle ground. Conversational but not rough. Carries a slight emotional charge — genuine puzzlement or frustration.
なんで — casual, everyday. The one friends use with each other.

The emotional coloring matters. どうして来なかったの？ (why didn't you come?) sounds like you actually want to know and are a little hurt. なんで来なかったの？ is more neutral — maybe annoyed, maybe just curious. どうして is the one that can carry disappointment without sounding confrontational.

meaning: to hang, put on (direct object, eg glasses)
lesson:
掛ける is one of the most overloaded verbs in Japanese. Its core meaning is "to hang" or "to apply something onto something else," but from that root it branches into a dozen specific uses. 壁に絵を掛ける (hang a picture on the wall). 眼鏡を掛ける (put on glasses — hang them on your face). 電話を掛ける (make a phone call — hang your voice on the line). 鍵を掛ける (lock a door — apply the lock). 声を掛ける (call out to someone — throw your voice at them).

The thread: you're placing something onto or over something else. Glasses onto your face. A lock onto a door. Your voice onto a person. Words onto a telephone line. Once you see 掛ける as "apply X to Y," the different uses stop feeling random.

The kanji 掛 has the hand radical 扌 — this is an action done with your hands — plus 卦, which is a phonetic component related to divination sticks being hung up. The hand physically hanging something: that's the image at the character's core.

Particles: 掛ける takes を for the thing being hung/applied, and often に for the surface or target. 壁に絵を掛ける. 友達に電話を掛ける.

Potential が掛けられる: が — what can be hung/applied becomes the subject.

Passive に掛けられる: に marks the agent. 夜中に電話を掛けられた (someone called me in the middle of the night — I suffered).

Causative に…を掛けさせる: に marks who you make do the hanging, を marks the thing.

一段 Ichidan他動詞 — pair: 掛かる (かかる)

Negativeかけないを

meaning: to turn, bend, curve (no direct object)
lesson:
曲がる is the verb of turning — streets, roads, your body. 右に曲がる (turn right). 角を曲がる (turn the corner). This is the verb you need for navigation: 次の信号を右に曲がってください (please turn right at the next traffic light). It's intransitive — the road curves, you turn, the wire bends. Nobody is bending it (that would be 曲げる, the transitive partner).

The kanji 曲 depicts something that was once straight and has been bent — an angular shape with a curve forced into it. It also means "song" or "musical piece" (この曲が好き, I like this song), which seems unrelated until you realize that melodies bend and curve too — a tune is a line that doesn't stay straight.

Particles: Two patterns. を marks the path or corner you turn at: 角を曲がる (turn the corner). This is movement-を, marking the space traversed, not a direct object. に marks the direction: 左に曲がる (turn left).

Passive に曲がられる: rare and unusual for this verb.

Causative を／に曲がらせる: making someone turn. 運転手にここで曲がらせた (I had the driver turn here).

The polite way to say stomach. 腹はら is the bare kanji reading — blunt, masculine, used in set expressions. お腹 wraps it in the honorific お and softens the reading to なか, making it the standard everyday word. You'd always say お腹 in polite company. 腹 is for tough-guy talk and idioms.

Two phrases you'll use constantly: お腹が空いた (I'm hungry — my stomach has emptied) and お腹がいっぱい (I'm full — my stomach is packed). Notice the verb 空すく — it means "to become empty," and the past tense 空いた describes the resulting state. Your stomach emptied and now it's in that state. Japanese frames hunger as an event that happened to your stomach, not a feeling you're experiencing.

The kanji 腹 has the meat/body radical 月 (which looks like "moon" but in body-part kanji represents flesh) plus 复, a phonetic element meaning "return" or "repeat." The belly is where things go and come back — where food enters and energy returns from.

Borrowed from English, but ミーティング occupies a specific niche that native Japanese words don't quite fill. Japanese already has 会議かいぎ for formal meetings — board meetings, committee meetings, anything with an agenda and minutes. ミーティング is lighter: a team check-in, a quick sync, a casual gathering to discuss something. It implies less hierarchy and less formality.

This split is common with loanwords. Japanese doesn't borrow English words randomly — it borrows them to fill gaps in nuance or register. 会議 feels corporate and official. ミーティング feels modern and approachable. A startup would more naturally schedule a ミーティング. A government ministry would hold a 会議. Same activity, different cultural packaging.

朝のミーティング (morning meeting). ミーティングがある (I have a meeting). ミーティングルーム (meeting room) — yet another layer of English bolted on.

嫌い looks like an い-adjective but isn't. It's a な-adjective: 嫌いな食べ物 (food I dislike), not 嫌い食べ物. The い at the end is a trap — it's part of the word's root, not the adjective ending. A few な-adjectives do this (きれい, 嫌い), and you just have to memorize which ones.

Grammatically, 嫌い works like 好き — the mirror image. The thing you dislike takes が: 野菜が嫌い (I dislike vegetables). Intensify it: 大嫌だいきらい (hate). The scale runs: 大好き → 好き → 嫌い → 大嫌い.

Saying 嫌い directly to someone is harsh. あなたが嫌い is a devastating sentence — romantic breakup territory. For softer rejection, Japanese has an arsenal of indirectness: ちょっと苦手にがて ("I'm not great with it"), あまり好きじゃない ("I don't really like it"). The culture of indirectness means 嫌い hits harder than its English equivalent.

Dictionaryきらい
Negativeきらいじゃない
Pastきらいだった
Past negきらいじゃなかった
Adverbきらいに
て-formきらいで

金 (metal, gold) + 曜日 = Metal-day = Friday. In the planetary day system, Friday belongs to Venus (just like in English — Friday comes from the Norse goddess Freya, associated with Venus). In East Asian five-element cosmology, Venus corresponds to metal. So 金曜日 and "Friday" both point to Venus through different cultural lenses.

金 by itself means gold or money (お金, おかね, money). The association makes Friday feel like payday, and in modern Japan it kind of is — 花金はなきん ("flower Friday") was a Bubble-era term for the celebration of Friday night after getting paid. The term has faded but the sentiment hasn't. Friday evening in any Japanese city is busy.

Mnemonic: Friday is kin-youbi. You kin-da want the weekend to start. Or: payday is on Friday, and you're making that 金 (money).

Pronounced exactly like いる (to exist/be, for animate things) — but completely different word, different kanji, different conjugation pattern. いる (to be) is ichidan. 要る (to need) is godan. In conversation you can't tell them apart by sound, only by context: お金が要る (I need money) vs. 猫がいる (there's a cat).

In practice, 要る is most commonly heard in its negative form: 要いらない (don't need it). At a shop: レシート要りますか？ (do you need a receipt?) → 要りません or the casual 要らない. The negative conjugation is the giveaway that this is godan, not ichidan — いない (not there) vs. いらない (don't need).

The kanji 要 means "essential" or "main point" — it shows up in 重要じゅうよう (important) and 必要ひつよう (necessary). When something is 要る, it's not just wanted — it's required.

Particles: 要る takes が — the needed thing is the subject. パスポートが要る (a passport is needed). Not を — the thing isn't an object you're acting on, it's a subject that's in a state of being necessary.

Passive/Causative: Rarely used for this verb. Japanese reaches for 必要とされる or 求められる when it needs a passive "is needed" construction.

ない is simultaneously the simplest and most important word you'll learn. It means "there isn't" or "doesn't exist" for inanimate things — the counterpart to ある. お金がない (there's no money). 時間がない (there's no time). But that's just its surface job.

The deeper role: ない is the negative ending bolted onto adjectives, verbs, and entire sentences. 食べない (don't eat). 高くない (not expensive). 静かじゃない (not quiet). Every negative statement in Japanese ends in ない or one of its conjugated forms. It's the word of absence, and it's everywhere.

Grammatically, ない behaves like an い-adjective. It conjugates: なかった (past), なくて (て-form), なければ (conditional). This means every negative verb is essentially an adjective at its tail — 食べない conjugates further as 食べなかった, 食べなくて, etc. Understanding that ない is an adjective, not a suffix, unlocks how Japanese negation works at every level.

Dictionaryない
Negative(N/A — already negative)
Pastなかった
Past neg(N/A)
Adverbなく
て-formなくて

Most Japanese colors aren't adjectives. You can say 赤い (red), 白い (white), 黒い (black), 青い (blue) — these ancient colors have い-adjective forms. But yellow had to hack its way in. The base word is the noun 黄色きいろ (yellow color), and someone stuck い on the end to force it into adjective shape: 黄色い. It works, everyone uses it, but it's structurally a noun with an adjective costume on.

Both forms coexist: 黄色い花 and 黄色の花 both mean "yellow flower" and both sound natural. 茶色ちゃいろい (brown) does the same trick. These are the only two colors that pulled off this noun-to-adjective conversion. Every other non-basic color stays as a noun with の: 緑の, 紫の, ピンクの.

The kanji 黄 is visually busy — it originally depicted the color of firelight or a particular type of jade. 色いろ means color. Together: yellow-color. Clean and literal.

Dictionaryきいろい
Negativeきいろくない
Pastきいろかった
Past negきいろくなかった
Adverbきいろく
て-formきいろくて

Same sound as 風かぜ (wind) — and that's not a coincidence. In traditional East Asian medicine, colds were caused by "wind" entering the body. 風邪 literally writes out as 風 (wind) + 邪 (evil, perverse) = evil wind. A cold is a malicious breeze that got inside you. The same logic exists in Chinese medicine, where 風邪 (fēngxié) refers to pathogenic wind. English has a faint echo of this too — we say you "catch" a cold, as if something airborne grabbed you.

風邪を引く (to catch a cold) — note the verb: 引く, to pull/draw. You don't "get" a cold in Japanese. You "draw" it in, like pulling something toward you. 風邪を引いた (I caught a cold). 風邪気味かぜぎみ (coming down with a cold — cold-ish).

In Japan, wearing a face mask when you're sick predates COVID by decades. Saying 風邪を引いたので (because I caught a cold...) is a socially acceptable reason for almost anything — canceling plans, leaving early, wearing a mask. The common cold does real social work in Japanese culture.

Two words pronounced やさしい. Two completely different kanji. This is the one about the heart.

優しい — kind, gentle, tender. The kanji 優 has the person radical and 憂 (grief, sorrow). A person who knows grief — who has felt sorrow and therefore treats others softly. Kindness born from understanding pain. That etymology is beautiful and worth remembering: 優しい people aren't kind because they're naive. They're kind because they know what hurting feels like.

易しい — easy, simple. The kanji 易 means "easy" or "change." Completely different meaning, same pronunciation.

Context always makes the distinction clear. 優しい人 (a kind person). 易しい問題 (an easy problem). In speech, you'll never confuse them because one describes people and the other describes tasks. But it's worth noting both exist so you don't freeze when you see やさしい written in hiragana and have to read the context to know which one it is.

Dictionaryやさしい
Negativeやさしくない
Pastやさしかった
Past negやさしくなかった
Adverbやさしく
て-formやさしくて
